segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Welcome along. Today we're looking at a new CARSI awareness course, Australian H5 Bird Flu Awareness for Restoration, IAQ and Facility Professionals. I'll be honest, when I first saw the title I thought, bird flu, that sounds alarming. Should I be worried?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: That's exactly the reaction the course is designed to steady. The short answer is no, and the whole tone here is calm and evidence-led. This isn't a crisis briefing. It's professional-boundary awareness training. It's for restoration, cleaning, indoor air quality, facility, strata and insurance-support teams who might get asked questions about a sick or dead bird on a site, and who need to know exactly what to do and, just as importantly, what not to do.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: So it's not veterinary training, or wildlife handling?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: Correct. It's not veterinary training, not wildlife handling, not public-health diagnosis, and it's not government biosecurity response training. It's awareness. It teaches you to communicate calmly, report correctly, protect your workers, stay within your scope, and document what you did.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Let's start with the official position, because that grounds everything. What are the authorities actually saying?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: Australia has reported H5 bird flu detections in wild birds, migratory seabirds in particular. The professional position is to follow Australian Government and Australian Centre for Disease Control advice, and the Centre for Disease Control's assessment is that the current risk to people in Australia is considered low. There's no evidence of infection in poultry or the wider agriculture industry. So we deliberately avoid words like outbreak, crisis or emergency, unless those are the exact words the authorities are using for the situation.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: That's a discipline in itself, isn't it, watching your language.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: It really is. Calm language builds trust; panic language damages credibility. The course is quite firm on that.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Okay, so imagine I'm on a site and there's a dead bird. What's the pathway?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: There's a simple sequence, and it's worth memorising. Do not touch it. Avoid the area. Keep children and pets away. Record the location and what you see. Take photos or video, but only if it's safe to do so. And then report it to the Emergency Animal Disease Hotline on 1800 675 888.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: 1800 675 888. Report first, don't touch, record the location, and call.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: That's the whole message in one line. And notice what you're not doing. You're not deciding whether the site is infected, and you're not declaring it safe either. You use neutral language: observed, reported, excluded from scope, referred to the authority.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: That leads into the professional's role. Where does a restoration or IAQ professional's job start and stop?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: The role is support, not authority replacement, that's the key phrase. You can assist with a lot: site notes and photo records, access control and exclusion-zone records, worker-safety planning, PPE record-keeping, cleaning records, checking product labels and safety data sheets, client-communication notes, reporting through RestoreAssist, and escalation to the correct authority.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: And the hard boundaries, the things you must not do?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: Five clear ones. You don't handle wildlife without authorisation. You don't diagnose avian influenza. You don't certify a site as disease-free. You don't override government, veterinary, wildlife or public-health authorities. And you don't claim that a particular product or a dry-fogging method is required unless there's written authority direction and product evidence to support it.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Say more about that last one, because products are where I'd imagine people can overreach.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: They can. CARSI's training position is clean first, disinfect second, document third. Any disinfection claim must never exceed the product label, the safety data sheet, the regulator's position or the evidence base. So, for example, hydrogen-peroxide dry fogging shouldn't be described as required by Australian Government control measures unless a written authority direction and product evidence back that up. Dry fogging can be discussed as a possible adjunct method, where it's legal, label-supported, risk-assessed, unoccupied where required, and done by trained operators after the source has been removed and the area cleaned. Product training isn't product hype.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Before we go on site at all, there's safety, presumably.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: Always. You start with a site-specific work health and safety risk assessment: what's been observed, whether the authorities have been contacted, whether the site is under direction, and whether the work is even within your scope. You set up clean and dirty zones to reduce cross-contamination. The course deliberately doesn't prescribe one universal PPE set, because PPE has to be matched to the task, the risk, the authority guidance, and the product label and safety data sheet. But your records should show what PPE was selected, who wore it, any breaches, and what was excluded from the job.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Which brings us to documentation, and I get the sense this is really the heart of it.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: It is. There's a line in the course I'd put on the wall: if it is not documented, it cannot be reviewed. The whole idea is that a client, an insurer, a supervisor or an authority can open your file and understand what was observed, what was reported, what was done, what wasn't done, and what evidence supported each decision. That's the documentation pathway RestoreAssist is built for. It turns the job from a memory into a defensible record.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: And staying calm isn't just good manners, it's part of the professional record too.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: Exactly. Measured, neutral, evidence-led communication is the professional standard here: on site, with clients, and on social media. You follow Australian Government advice, you point people to 1800 675 888, and you're clear that your role is documentation, worker safety and professional support. You don't replace the authorities.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: If someone finishes this course, what have they actually got?

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: A calm, correct default. They know the official message, the reporting pathway, where their role begins and ends, how to protect their workers, and how to document it all so it stands up to review. It's awareness training, and the certificate says exactly that: not veterinary, medical, wildlife-handling or government biosecurity response training.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Report first, don't touch, record, and call 1800 675 888. That's the one to remember.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: That's the one. Calm, in scope, and documented.

segments.speaker: A
segments.text: Lovely. Thanks for walking through it.

segments.speaker: B
segments.text: My pleasure.
